article_title: Salesforce Disables Klue Battlecards Integration After OAuth Token Theft Drains CRM Data From Customers Including Huntress
summary: Accurate breach report; every quote, number, name and date verified against snapshots; Icarus framing correctly confined to BleepingComputer with honest attribution caveat. Allowlist warning is a coverage gap, not a content error.

findings.message: Source fetched via Archive.org fallback (original returned 200)

content_preview.title: Salesforce Disables Klue Battlecards Integration After OAuth Token Theft Drains CRM Data From Customers Including Huntress
content_preview.summary: Attackers abused a compromised legacy Klue credential to mint Salesforce OAuth tokens and pull CRM records over roughly 24 hours. Salesforce disabled the Battlecards app on June 17.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Salesforce has disabled the integration for Klue, a competitive-intelligence platform whose Battlecards app syncs sales data with customer CRM systems, after attackers used stolen OAuth tokens to siphon CRM records from connected organizations. Salesforce posted the status update on June 17, 2026, stating that it had "disabled the connection between the Klue Battlecards app ... as part of our response to a recent security incident," according to [Salesforce Ben](https://www.salesfor...

recommendations: Consider adding reliaquest.com, bankinfosecurity.com, govinfosecurity.com, salesforceben.com and cybersecuritynews.com to config/source_allowlist.txt — all are established security/industry outlets.

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (673 words, within the 400-1200 News range). Clear Overview / What We Know / What We Don't Know / Analysis arc. Technical depth (REST API endpoints, urllib UA, QueryMore pagination) is precise without overclaiming.
editor_notes.source_verification: Personally read all 6 gzipped snapshots in sources/2026-06/salesforce-disables-klue-battlecards-integration-after-oauth-token-theft-drains-crm-data-from-customers-including-huntress/. source-0 (ReliaQuest): confirmed the 'authenticated to targets' Klue integration service accounts...' quote, /services/data/v59.0/sobjects and /services/data/v59.0/query endpoints, Python-urllib UA, QueryMore cursor, 'roughly 24 hours', 'nearly a thousand queries in 15 minutes', 'over 6 hours', 'volume and pacing point to bulk data retrieval' and the ShinyHunters/UNC6395 no-confirm-no-rule-out language — all verbatim. Searched 'Icarus' in source-0: NOT FOUND, confirming ReliaQuest did NOT attribute to Icarus. source-1 (BleepingComputer): confirmed Icarus framing originates solely here — launched ~April 2026, leak site, Huntress as a victim. source-2 (BankInfoSecurity): Jason Smith quotes ('compromised legacy credential associated with an integration service', 'obtain OAuth tokens...connected customer environments', 'immediately took steps to contain...removing unauthorized code') verbatim; June 12 detection and June 19 confirmation dates confirmed; full suspended-integration list (Chorus, Clari, Gong, Google Drive, HubSpot, SharePoint, Slack App, Zoom) confirmed. source-3 (GovInfoSecurity): Huntress data quote ('business contacts, price quotes...no threat data, passwords, payment card information or engineering data') verbatim. source-4 (SalesforceBen): Salesforce status quotes verbatim including the direct 'This issue is limited to Klue's app connection...' line; June 17 date and Salesloft Drift/Gainsight 2025 parallel confirmed. source-5 (CyberSecurityNews, Archive.org fallback): UNC6395 tooling contrast (python-requests/Salesforce-CLI/Tor vs Python-urllib/data-center hosting) confirmed.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every direct quote, number, name and date traces verbatim to a cited source. No fabricated specifics detected.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral throughout. No sensationalism; attribution uncertainty is presented honestly rather than asserting a culprit.
editor_notes.originality: New, distinct event. A prior June article covers the Carnival/ShinyHunters social-engineering breach (different company, different vector) and another covers a ShinyHunters Oracle PeopleSoft zero-day. This is the Klue OAuth-integration incident — genuinely new, no duplication.
editor_notes.icarus_attribution_check: The 'Icarus' threat-actor framing is correctly kept OUT of the headline, summary and lead. It appears only in 'What We Don't Know', attributed solely to BleepingComputer, and explicitly flagged with the caveat that ReliaQuest found no evidence confirming involvement by any group of that name. Verified ReliaQuest's own snapshot contains no mention of Icarus. Attribution uncertainty (ShinyHunters/UNC6395 unconfirmed) is presented honestly.
recommendations: Add the five non-allowlisted security/industry domains to config/source_allowlist.txt; all are reputable.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE. Accurate, neutral, well-sourced breach report. The script's auto-verdict of APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS rested solely on the allowlist warning — a source-coverage gap, not a content error. There is no misattribution, fabricated specific, or paraphrased-as-quote issue that a public corrections record could honestly document, so no corrections file is warranted. Overriding to APPROVE.
